overviewGeneration:
Today is {today}. You are writing the "{groupLabel}" intelligence overview for {scopeLabel}, current as of today.
Everything you write must reflect the present moment — use the most recent information available and never present events, dates, awards, releases, or rankings from an earlier year as if they were current. If you are unsure whether a fact is still current, search for it rather than relying on memory.
SPECIFICITY: every sentence must name a specific event, person, organization, place, number, or date. Omit any claim you cannot make specific — never write filler like "tensions remain high", "various factors", "a natural disaster", "major developments", or "some experts". A shorter section containing only concrete facts is the correct outcome; vague padding is a failure.
TENSE: state events as they actually stand as of today — if something has already happened, say so in the past tense; never describe a past event as upcoming or still in progress.
Produce EXACTLY the sections listed below, in this order. Each section MUST start with "## " followed by the section name copied exactly, on its own line. Under each header write 2-4 sentences of plain prose answering that section's brief, then up to 2 key takeaways as "- " bullet lines. No other headers, no preamble before the first section, no closing remarks. Be accurate and specific — do not invent.

Sections:
{sections}
builderGeneration:
Today is {today}. Write an in-depth, polished "{groupLabel}" insight on {scopeLabel}, current as of today.
{builderGuidance}
Structure it with a few clear "## " section headings of YOUR choosing that best fit this topic (aim for 3-5 sections) — there is no fixed template; let the topic dictate the structure. Under each heading write tight, specific prose; you may add up to 3 "- " bullets where a list genuinely helps.
SPECIFICITY: every sentence must name a specific event, person, organization, place, number, or date. Omit any claim you cannot make specific — never write filler like "tensions remain high", "various factors", "major developments", or "some experts". A shorter section of only concrete facts is the right outcome; vague padding is a failure.
CURRENT FACTS — CRITICAL: today is {today} and your training data is OUT OF DATE for anything that changes over time. Treat your memory of any current office-holder, leader, price, ranking, standing, or count as STALE and probably wrong. NEVER name the specific current holder of a political office or leadership position (president, prime minister, head of state, CEO, chair, champion, etc.) from memory — the person your training associates with that role has very likely changed. You may name a current office-holder ONLY if a headline provided below or your live search result explicitly confirms who holds it as of {today}; otherwise refer to the office GENERICALLY ("the U.S. president", "the sitting administration", "the country's current leadership", "the company's CEO") rather than guessing a name. The same applies to current prices, standings, and counts — verify them or speak in general terms. Stating that someone still holds a position they have left (e.g. a former president or CEO described as current) is a serious, disqualifying error.
TENSE: state events as they actually stand as of today — describe past events in the past tense and never present a past event as upcoming or still in progress. Do not invent.
FORMAT: begin directly with the first "## " heading — no document title, no preamble before it, and no closing remarks after the last section.

builderGuidance.discover: FOCUS — Get Caught Up: catch the reader up on {topic} as of right now. Open by leading with the single biggest, most recent development, then cover the key news, moves, and announcements of the past week or two and what is actively unfolding. Range ACROSS {topic} — pull in several different stories, players, and sub-areas, not just one company or thread. This is time-sensitive — prioritise the newest concrete events; keep any forward-looking roadmaps or rumours brief (a line, not a section).
builderGuidance.topic-specific: FOCUS — Deep Dive: go beneath the headlines on {topic} in two moves. FIRST, pick the few most important current developments and active sub-areas and go DEEP on each — how it actually works, the key players and forces, the real numbers and details, and what is distinctive or genuinely contested (fewer threads than a news roundup, each explored with real depth). THEN step back and reason about what it all means — the central tensions and trade-offs, the credible competing perspectives, the real risks and red flags, and an honest big-picture takeaway. This is current and detailed — NOT an evergreen primer (do not explain the field's fundamentals or history from scratch) and NOT a quick news list. Span several facets of {topic} across multiple players and sub-areas; use specific examples to support the analysis, not as the subject itself.
builderGuidance.learn: FOCUS — 101 Resources: a clear, ground-up primer on {topic} for someone starting from scratch. Cover the fundamentals, the essential concepts and terms, the key people and organisations to know, and the background needed to follow the topic. Keep it EVERGREEN and timeless — a reader should find it just as accurate in six months. Do NOT include this week's news, recent events, results, transfers, appointments, signings, or announcements; those belong in the other sections, not the primer. You MAY state durable present-day facts (e.g. who currently holds a major office or governs the sport), but verify those rather than recalling them from memory.
